chunk_summary: Management of urinary tract infections (UTIs) in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) emphasizes a culture-first approach, proper specimen collection, tailored antibiotic use, and reassessment within 48–72 hours if no improvement is seen.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of a culture-first approach in managing UTIs in SAM.
Learn proper urine specimen collection techniques for different age groups.
Identify appropriate empiric antibiotic regimens based on illness severity and local antibiograms.
Recognize the need for reassessment and follow-up in cases of treatment failure or recurrent UTI risk.
key_facts: In SAM, UTI should be suspected in cases of fever without focus, persistent hypothermia, vomiting, or poor weight gain.
Urine culture should be obtained before starting antibiotics whenever feasible.
Specimen collection methods: suprapubic aspiration or catheterization for non-toilet-trained children, midstream clean-catch for toilet-trained children.
Empiric antibiotics: oral cephalosporin (e.g., cefixime) or amoxicillin/clavulanate for stable children; parenteral cefotaxime or ceftriaxone for toxic or vomiting children.
Treatment duration: 7–10 days in SAM unless culture results suggest otherwise.
Reassess within 48–72 hours if no improvement and investigate for urinary obstruction, renal anomaly, or alternative diagnoses.
reference_quote: Obtain urine for urinalysis and culture before antibiotics whenever feasible. Use a properly collected specimen: suprapubic aspiration or catheterization in non-toilet-trained children, and midstream clean-catch in toilet-trained children.
document_key_topics: Culture-first approach in UTI management for SAM
Reassessment and follow-up in UTI treatment
